title: Static API Specification
description: The 'static files ARE the API' pattern. No server. No backend. Git repos + raw CDN = API. The frame IS the API call. The repo IS the server. Git IS the database.
author: kody-w (Wildhaven Homes LLC)
license: Specification is public. Implementations may be proprietary.

spec.overview: A Static API is a git repository containing structured JSON files served via raw CDN (GitHub Pages, raw.githubusercontent.com, or any static host). Clients consume data by polling static URLs. Engines produce data by generating frames, rebuilding bundles, committing, and pushing. No server process runs. The CDN IS the server. Git IS the database. The commit log IS the audit trail.

philosophy.core_insight: If your data changes less than once per minute, you don't need a server.
philosophy.the_frame_is_the_api_call: Each frame generated by the engine is equivalent to a POST to a traditional API. The frame is written to JSON, committed to git, and pushed. The CDN serves the new state. Consumers poll and receive the update. The frame IS the API call.
philosophy.the_repo_is_the_server: A git repository with static JSON files, served via raw CDN, is functionally equivalent to a REST API. GET /frames.json returns all data. GET /latest.json returns the heartbeat. GET /manifest.json returns the index. The repo IS the server.
philosophy.git_is_the_database: Every commit is a transaction. Every push is a deployment. Every branch is an environment. Every tag is a release. The commit log is the audit trail. Git IS the database.
philosophy.zero_ops: No server to provision. No database to manage. No load balancer to configure. No SSL to renew. No monitoring to set up. No scaling to worry about. Push JSON to git. The world can read it.

frames.json.description: The complete data bundle. Contains all frames (or the current window of frames). This is the primary data endpoint.
frames.json.content: Array of frame objects, each with sol, timestamp, environmental readings, colony state, scores, and chain verification data.
frames.json.size: Typically 100KB-5MB depending on frame count and detail level.
frames.json.update_frequency: Every time the engine generates new frames and pushes.
frames.json.consumers: Dashboards, viewers, AI agents, leaderboards, analysis tools.

latest.json.description: The heartbeat endpoint. Contains metadata about the most recent frame and the current state of the API.

content.hash: SHA-256 hash of frames.json for integrity verification
content.engine: Engine ID that produced this data

latest.json.update_frequency: Same as frames.json — updated atomically in the same commit.
latest.json.consumers: Polling clients check this first. If sol or hash changed, fetch the full bundle.

manifest.json.description: The index endpoint. Lists all available data files, their sizes, hashes, and relationships. Enables discovery.

content.files: Array of {path, size, hash, description} objects for each data file
content.endpoints: Map of endpoint names to their paths and descriptions
content.federation: Links to other Static API repos in the network
content.spec_version: Version of this spec the API conforms to

manifest.json.update_frequency: Every push. Rebuilt by the engine alongside frames.json.
manifest.json.consumers: Crawlers, federation discovery, tooling, documentation generators.

client_pattern.overview: Clients consume the Static API by polling latest.json, diffing for changes, and fetching the full bundle only when data has changed.
steps: 2. Compare latest.sol and latest.hash to locally cached values
3. If unchanged: done. Wait and poll again.
4. If changed: fetch frames.json (the full bundle)
5. Diff new frames against locally cached frames to find new/changed frames
6. Process new frames (update UI, run analysis, feed to AI agent)
7. Cache the new frames.json and latest.json locally
client_pattern.polling_interval: Recommended: 30-60 seconds for active consumers. 5-15 minutes for passive consumers. Adjust based on expected update frequency.
client_pattern.code_example: (pseudo) while (true) { latest = fetch(LATEST_URL); if (latest.hash !== cache.hash) { frames = fetch(FRAMES_URL); newFrames = diff(frames, cache.frames); process(newFrames); cache = { hash: latest.hash, frames }; } sleep(POLL_INTERVAL); }

engine_pattern.overview: Engines produce data by generating frames, rebuilding the bundle, and pushing to git. Each push is a deployment.
steps: 1. Generate new frame(s) from simulation/data source
2. Append frame(s) to the frames array
5. Rebuild latest.json with new sol, timestamp, frameCount, hash
6. Rebuild manifest.json with updated file sizes and hashes
10. CDN cache invalidates (or client polls past cache TTL)
engine_pattern.atomicity: All three files must be updated in the same commit. This ensures clients never see a latest.json that points to a stale frames.json.

browser_localstorage.description: Clients cache frames.json and latest.json in browser localStorage (or IndexedDB for large bundles).
browser_localstorage.strategy: Store the full frames array and the latest.hash. On next poll, compare hashes before fetching.
browser_localstorage.size_limit: localStorage: ~5MB. IndexedDB: ~50MB+. For bundles > 5MB, use IndexedDB.

http_caching.description: Use standard HTTP caching headers on raw CDN URLs.
http_caching.etag: Send If-None-Match with the previous ETag. If the file hasn't changed, the CDN returns 304 Not Modified (zero bandwidth).
http_caching.if_modified_since: Send If-Modified-Since with the last fetch timestamp. Same 304 behavior.
http_caching.cache_control: GitHub raw URLs have ~5 minute cache TTL. GitHub Pages allows custom Cache-Control headers.
http_caching.tip: For real-time needs, use GitHub Pages with short Cache-Control. For batch processing, raw.githubusercontent.com with ETag is sufficient.

cdn_behavior.github_raw: raw.githubusercontent.com — 5 minute cache, no custom headers, supports ETag/If-Modified-Since. No rate limit on GET (unlike API).
cdn_behavior.github_pages: Custom domain or {user}.github.io/{repo} — configurable cache, HTTPS, custom headers via _headers file.
cdn_behavior.any_static_host: Netlify, Vercel, Cloudflare Pages, S3+CloudFront — all work. The spec is host-agnostic.

federation.overview: Each git repo is an independent API instance. Federation is discovery across repos.
federation.discovery: A crawler reads manifest.json from known repos. Each manifest.json contains a 'federation' field linking to other repos.
federation.aggregation: A federation hub aggregates latest.json from all known repos into a single directory. Clients can poll the hub to discover all active API instances.
federation.independence: Each repo is sovereign. No central server. No central authority. A repo going offline doesn't affect others. A repo being malicious is detected by chain verification and excluded.
federation.pattern: Like RSS: each blog has a feed. Each reader discovers feeds. No central feed server. The Static API is RSS for structured data.

sha256_hashes.description: Every frames.json has its SHA-256 hash published in latest.json and manifest.json.
sha256_hashes.verification: Clients compute the hash of the fetched frames.json and compare against latest.hash. Mismatch = tampered data or CDN corruption. Reject.
sha256_hashes.chain: Each frame contains the hash of the previous frame, forming a hash chain. Verify the chain to detect inserted, removed, or reordered frames.

engine_signatures.description: Engines sign frames with their private key. The public key is published in manifest.json.
engine_signatures.format: Ed25519 signature over the frame's canonical JSON representation.
engine_signatures.verification: Clients verify the signature using the engine's published public key. Invalid signature = untrusted frame.

chain_verification.description: Frames form a hash chain. Each frame's hash includes the previous frame's hash.
chain_verification.integrity: Verify the chain is unbroken from genesis to latest. Any break = tampering detected.
chain_verification.cross_repo: Anchor frames include hashes from other repos' chains, creating cross-repo verification.

security.no_execution: Static APIs serve data only. No code execution on the server (there is no server). Client-side code runs in the browser sandbox. The attack surface is: can someone push bad JSON to git? Standard git access controls apply.

anti_patterns.dont_use_github_api: GitHub REST/GraphQL API has rate limits (60/hr unauthenticated, 5000/hr authenticated). Raw URLs and GitHub Pages have NO rate limit for GET requests. Always use raw URLs.
anti_patterns.dont_serve_individual_frames: Serving one file per frame creates thousands of HTTP requests. Bundle frames into frames.json. One request gets all data.
anti_patterns.dont_skip_latest: Without latest.json, clients must fetch the full bundle every poll to check for changes. latest.json is < 1KB and prevents unnecessary large fetches.
anti_patterns.dont_break_atomicity: Updating frames.json without updating latest.json (or vice versa) creates inconsistency. Always update all three files in one commit.

size_guidelines.frames_json: < 5 MB for localStorage caching, < 50 MB for IndexedDB, < 100 MB for git comfort
size_guidelines.total_repo: < 1 GB. Use git LFS or archive old frames if approaching this.
size_guidelines.trimming: Keep a rolling window of frames in the active bundle. Archive older frames to separate files or branches.

vs_rest_api.rest: Server process, database, auth middleware, rate limiting, monitoring, scaling, ops team
vs_rest_api.static: JSON files in git, CDN serves them, no process, no ops, infinite scale for reads

vs_graphql.graphql: Server with schema, resolvers, query planning, N+1 optimization, subscription infra
vs_graphql.static: Pre-computed bundle. No query flexibility, but zero latency planning and zero server cost

vs_websocket.websocket: Persistent connection, server memory per client, reconnection logic, scaling complexity
vs_websocket.static: Polling. Slightly higher latency. Zero server state. Infinitely scalable. Works offline.

comparison.when_static_wins: Data changes less than once per minute. Read-heavy (1000:1 read:write). Data is public. Team is small. Budget is zero. You want the API to exist for 10 years without maintenance.
comparison.when_static_loses: Real-time requirements (< 1s latency). Write-heavy. Private data requiring auth per request. Complex query patterns requiring server-side filtering.
